title: CMMC Model Construct and Implementation — Level 1 Practices

examine.definition: Reviewing, inspecting, observing, studying, or analyzing assessment objects including documents, mechanisms, or activities.
examine.plain_terms: Looking at stuff — policies, configs, logs, diagrams, records.

interview.definition: Holding discussions with individuals or groups to facilitate understanding, achieve clarification, or obtain evidence.
interview.plain_terms: Talking to people — admins, security staff, users, management.

test.definition: Exercising assessment objects under specified conditions to compare actual with expected behavior.
test.plain_terms: Making something do the thing and watching what happens — demonstrations, live checks.

assessment_methods.key_relationship: Interview = belief, Examine = documentation, Test = reality. All three together give a complete picture.

MET.definition: All applicable objectives for the practice are satisfied based on evidence.
MET.evidence_requirement: Must be in final form. Drafts, working papers, and unapproved policies do NOT count.
special_cases: Enduring Exceptions documented in the system security plan score as MET
Temporary deficiencies properly documented in an operational plan of action with milestones score as MET

NOT_MET.definition: One or more objectives of the practice are not satisfied.
NOT_MET.critical_rule: ONE NOT MET objective = the entire practice is NOT MET. No partial credit.

NOT_APPLICABLE.definition: The requirement does not apply at the time of assessment. Must be documented with explanation.
NOT_APPLICABLE.scoring: Treated as equivalent to MET for scoring purposes.

level1_practices.name: Authorized Access Control
level1_practices.requirement: Limit information system access to authorized users, processes acting on behalf of authorized users, or devices (including other information systems).
level1_practices.plain_terms: Only authorized users, processes, and devices can access the system. No unauthorized people, no rogue devices.
assessment_objectives: authorized users are identified
processes acting on behalf of authorized users are identified
devices authorized to connect are identified
system access is limited to authorized users
system access is limited to authorized processes
system access is limited to authorized devices
evidence_examples: Records of terminated employee account removal

level1_practices.name: Transaction and Function Control
level1_practices.requirement: Limit information system access to the types of transactions and functions that authorized users are permitted to execute.
level1_practices.plain_terms: Once you're in, what you can DO is also limited. Least privilege — users only get access to what their role requires.
level1_practices.key_principle: Control WHAT authorized users can do once in
assessment_objectives: types of transactions and functions authorized users are permitted to execute are defined
system access is limited to defined types of transactions and functions

level1_practices.name: External Connections
level1_practices.requirement: Verify and control/limit connections to and use of external information systems.
level1_practices.plain_terms: Know what external connections exist, verify they're authorized, and control/limit them. Includes cloud services, vendor networks, personal devices.
level1_practices.key_principle: Control external CONNECTIONS to your system
assessment_objectives: connections to external systems are identified
use of external systems is identified
connections to external systems are verified
use of external systems is verified
connections to external systems are controlled/limited
use of external systems is controlled/limited
evidence_examples: Documented agreements with external system owners

level1_practices.name: Control Public Information
level1_practices.requirement: Control information posted or processed on publicly accessible information systems.
level1_practices.plain_terms: If you run anything public-facing, you need controls to ensure FCI doesn't end up there. Designated individuals, review process before posting, mechanisms to remove FCI if discovered.
level1_practices.key_principle: Control PUBLIC-facing systems to prevent FCI exposure
assessment_objectives: individuals authorized to post on publicly accessible systems are identified
procedures to ensure FCI is not posted on publicly accessible systems are identified
a review process is in place prior to posting content
content is reviewed to ensure it does not include FCI
mechanisms are in place to remove and address improper posting of FCI
evidence_examples: List of users authorized to post publicly
Documented approval chain for public release

level1_practices.name: Identification
level1_practices.requirement: Identify information system users, processes acting on behalf of users, or devices.
level1_practices.plain_terms: Every user, process, and device needs a unique identifier. No shared accounts, no generic logins. Activity must be traceable to a specific identity.
level1_practices.key_principle: Claiming an identity — I am Sean (username)
assessment_objectives: system users are identified
processes acting on behalf of users are identified
devices accessing the system are identified
evidence_examples: List of system accounts with unique user IDs
level1_practices.note: IA.L1-b.1.v provides vetted identity that supports the access control mechanism in AC.L1-b.1.i

level1_practices.name: Authentication
level1_practices.requirement: Authenticate the identities of those users, processes, or devices as a prerequisite to allowing access.
level1_practices.plain_terms: Prove who you are before getting access. Passwords are the baseline. Default credentials must be changed immediately — they are publicly known and a significant security risk.
level1_practices.key_principle: Proving an identity — here is my password/credential
assessment_objectives: identity of each user is authenticated as a prerequisite to system access
identity of each process acting on behalf of a user is authenticated
identity of each device is authenticated as a prerequisite to system access
level1_practices.exam_trap: Default credentials are a known risk — devices ship with publicly documented default usernames and passwords that must be changed before deployment.

level1_practices.name: Media Disposal
level1_practices.requirement: Sanitize or destroy information system media containing FCI before disposal or release for reuse.
level1_practices.plain_terms: FCI media must be sanitized (cleaned for reuse) or destroyed (made permanently unreadable) before disposal or reuse. Media includes paper, USB drives, CDs, DVDs, hard drives, mobile phones.
level1_practices.key_principle: FCI must be unrecoverable before media leaves your control
assessment_objectives: system media containing FCI is sanitized or destroyed before disposal
system media containing FCI is sanitized before it is released for reuse

key_references.supplementary: NIST SP 800-88 Guidelines for Media Sanitization

level1_practices.distinction: Sanitize = remove FCI, preserve media for reuse. Destroy = physically destroy, media unusable. Choose sanitize for reuse, destroy for disposal.

level1_practices.name: Limit Physical Access
level1_practices.requirement: Limit physical access to organizational information systems, equipment, and operating environments to authorized individuals.
level1_practices.plain_terms: Only authorized people can physically access systems, equipment, and the spaces where they live. Locks, badges, key cards. Output devices like printers must be placed where they don't expose data.
level1_practices.key_principle: Control WHO can physically access systems and spaces
assessment_objectives: authorized individuals allowed physical access are identified
physical access to organizational systems is limited to authorized individuals
physical access to equipment is limited to authorized individuals
physical access to operating environments is limited to authorized individuals
evidence_examples: Physical access credentials issued to appropriate personnel
level1_practices.practical_note: CMMC doesn't mandate specific solutions. A locked room and a key list satisfies this for a small company.

level1_practices.name: Manage Visitors and Physical Access
level1_practices.requirement: Escort visitors and monitor visitor activity; maintain audit logs of physical access; and control and manage physical access devices.
level1_practices.plain_terms: Three things: (1) visitors must be escorted at all times — no exceptions even for people you know; (2) physical access must be logged — paper or electronic; (3) physical access devices (keys, badges, cards) must be inventoried and managed.
level1_practices.key_principle: Track and manage HOW physical access occurs
assessment_objectives: visitors are escorted
visitor activity is monitored
audit logs of physical access are maintained
physical access devices are identified
physical access devices are controlled
physical access devices are managed
evidence_examples: Records of access revocation when personnel depart
level1_practices.exam_trap: Individuals with permanent physical access credentials are NOT considered visitors. The escort requirement applies to everyone without permanent credentials — even people you know personally.

level1_practices.name: Boundary Protection
level1_practices.requirement: Monitor, control, and protect organizational communications at the external boundaries and key internal boundaries of the information systems.
level1_practices.plain_terms: Three actions (monitor, control, protect) at two locations (external boundary and key internal boundaries). Firewalls and web proxies are the primary tools. Deny by default, allow by exception.
level1_practices.key_principle: Protect the network perimeter and key internal dividing lines
assessment_objectives: external system boundary is defined
key internal system boundaries are defined
communications are monitored at the external boundary
communications are monitored at key internal boundaries
communications are controlled at the external boundary
communications are controlled at key internal boundaries
communications are protected at the external boundary
communications are protected at key internal boundaries

level1_practices.name: Public-Access System Separation
level1_practices.requirement: Implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are physically or logically separated from internal networks.
level1_practices.plain_terms: Public-facing systems (websites, portals, VPN gateways) must be on a separate network from internal systems where FCI lives. This is a DMZ. Traffic from the DMZ to internal networks is blocked by default.
level1_practices.key_principle: Public-facing systems must be isolated from FCI systems
assessment_objectives: publicly accessible system components are identified
subnetworks for publicly accessible components are physically or logically separated from internal networks
evidence_examples: Firewall rules blocking DMZ-to-internal traffic by default
level1_practices.key_term: DMZ — Demilitarized Zone. A subnetwork physically or logically separated from internal networks. Can be N/A if no publicly accessible systems exist within scope.
level1_practices.distinction_from_b1x: b.1.x = protect all network boundaries with monitoring and controls. b.1.xi = specifically separate public-facing components into their own isolated subnetwork.

level1_practices.name: Flaw Remediation
level1_practices.requirement: Identify, report, and correct information system flaws in a timely manner.
level1_practices.plain_terms: Patch management. Three actions (identify, report, correct) must all happen within organization-defined timeframes. Timeframes must be documented. This covers patches, service packs, hot fixes.
level1_practices.key_principle: Find flaws, report them, fix them — on a defined schedule
assessment_objectives: time within which to identify system flaws is specified
system flaws are identified within the specified time frame
time within which to report system flaws is specified
system flaws are reported within the specified time frame
time within which to correct system flaws is specified
system flaws are corrected within the specified time frame
evidence_examples: Patch management policy with defined timeframes
List of installed patches and update history
Change control records for security updates

level1_practices.name: Malicious Code Protection
level1_practices.requirement: Provide protection from malicious code at appropriate locations within organizational information systems.
level1_practices.plain_terms: Deploy anti-malware protection at designated locations — entry/exit points, workstations, email servers, mobile devices. Must identify where protection is needed and actually provide it there.
level1_practices.key_principle: Deploy malware protection at the right places
assessment_objectives: designated locations for malicious code protection are identified
protection from malicious code at designated locations is provided
malicious_code_types: Virus — damages, steals, or changes data
Trojan Horse — malware disguised as legitimate software
Ransomware — blocks access or threatens to publish data unless ransom paid
evidence_examples: Anti-malware software deployed on identified systems

level1_practices.name: Update Malicious Code Protection
level1_practices.requirement: Update malicious code protection mechanisms when new releases are available.
level1_practices.plain_terms: Keep your antivirus and anti-malware definitions current. Malware evolves constantly — outdated protection is essentially no protection. Configure automatic updates.
level1_practices.key_principle: Keep malware protection up to date
assessment_objectives: malicious code protection mechanisms are updated when new releases are available
evidence_examples: Records of malicious code protection updates

level1_practices.name: System and File Scanning
level1_practices.requirement: Perform periodic scans of the information system and real-time scans of files from external sources as files are downloaded, opened, or executed.
level1_practices.plain_terms: Two types of scanning: (1) periodic scans of the whole system on a defined schedule; (2) real-time scans whenever files from external sources are downloaded, opened, or executed. USB drives count as external sources.
level1_practices.key_principle: Actively scan for malware on a schedule and in real time
assessment_objectives: frequency for malicious code scans is defined
malicious code scans are performed with the defined frequency
real-time malicious code scans of files from external sources are performed as files are downloaded, opened, or executed
evidence_examples: Evidence USB/removable media is included in external sources definition
level1_practices.distinction_from_b1xiii_b1xiv: b.1.xiii = deploy protection. b.1.xiv = keep it updated. b.1.xv = actively use it to scan.

AC.theme: Control who gets in and what they can do

MP.theme: Protect FCI when disposing of or reusing media

PE.theme: Control and track physical access to systems and spaces

SC.theme: Protect network boundaries and isolate public-facing systems

SI.theme: Keep systems patched, protected from malware, and actively scanned

flashcards.question: How many Level 1 practices are there and what is their source document?
flashcards.answer: 15 practices, derived from FAR Clause 52.204-21.

flashcards.question: What does Level 1 protect — FCI or CUI?
flashcards.answer: FCI — Federal Contract Information. CUI is protected at Level 2.

flashcards.question: What are the three assessment methods and what does each tell you?
flashcards.answer: Examine = what documentation says is happening. Interview = what people believe is happening. Test = what is actually happening.

flashcards.question: What happens if one assessment objective within a practice is NOT MET?
flashcards.answer: The entire practice is scored NOT MET. There is no partial credit at the practice level.

flashcards.question: Can a draft policy be used as evidence for a MET finding?
flashcards.answer: No. Evidence must be in final form. Drafts, working papers, and unapproved policies do not count.

flashcards.question: How does NOT APPLICABLE score in a CMMC assessment?
flashcards.answer: Equivalent to MET for scoring purposes. Must be documented with an explanation of why it doesn't apply.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between AC.L1-b.1.i and AC.L1-b.1.ii?
flashcards.answer: b.1.i controls WHO gets access to the system. b.1.ii controls WHAT authorized users can do once inside — least privilege principle.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between Identification and Authentication?
flashcards.answer: Identification is claiming an identity (username). Authentication is proving it (password/credential). IA.L1-b.1.v = identification, IA.L1-b.1.vi = authentication.

flashcards.question: Why are default credentials a significant security risk?
flashcards.answer: Default credentials are often publicly documented by manufacturers — attackers can look them up rather than needing to brute force. They must be changed before deployment.

flashcards.question: What types of media does MP.L1-b.1.vii apply to?
flashcards.answer: All media digital and non-digital: paper, USB drives, CDs, DVDs, hard drives, mobile phones, tapes. If it stores information, it counts.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between sanitizing and destroying media?
flashcards.answer: Sanitizing removes FCI while preserving the media for reuse. Destroying physically destroys the media making it permanently unreadable. Choose sanitize for reuse, destroy for disposal.

flashcards.question: What is the visitor escort rule under PE.L1-b.1.ix?
flashcards.answer: All visitors must be escorted at all times — no exceptions, even for people you know personally. Only individuals with permanent physical access credentials are exempt.

flashcards.question: What three things does PE.L1-b.1.ix require?
flashcards.answer: 1. Escort visitors and monitor their activity. 2. Maintain audit logs of physical access. 3. Control and manage physical access devices (keys, badges, cards).

flashcards.question: What is the difference between SC.L1-b.1.x and SC.L1-b.1.xi?
flashcards.answer: b.1.x protects all network boundaries with monitoring and controls. b.1.xi specifically requires public-facing systems to be isolated in a separate subnetwork (DMZ) away from internal FCI systems.

flashcards.question: What is a DMZ and when is SC.L1-b.1.xi NOT APPLICABLE?
flashcards.answer: A DMZ is a subnetwork physically or logically separated from internal networks. b.1.xi is N/A if no publicly accessible systems exist within the CMMC assessment scope.

flashcards.question: What three actions does SI.L1-b.1.xii require and what must be defined for each?
flashcards.answer: Identify, report, and correct system flaws. Each must happen within organization-defined timeframes that are documented. No specific timeframe is mandated — but timeframes must exist.

flashcards.question: What are the four types of malicious code listed in the Level 1 Assessment Guide?
flashcards.answer: Virus, Spyware, Trojan Horse, Ransomware.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between SI.L1-b.1.xiii, b.1.xiv, and b.1.xv?
flashcards.answer: b.1.xiii = deploy malware protection at designated locations. b.1.xiv = keep that protection updated. b.1.xv = actively use it — periodic system scans and real-time scans of external files.

flashcards.question: What two types of scanning does SI.L1-b.1.xv require?
flashcards.answer: Periodic scans of the whole system on a defined schedule, AND real-time scans of files from external sources as they are downloaded, opened, or executed. USB drives count as external sources.

flashcards.question: Which Level 1 domain has the most practices and what is its theme?
flashcards.answer: AC (Access Control) and SI (System and Information Integrity) both have 4 practices. AC controls access; SI keeps systems healthy and protected from malware.

flashcards.question: What does AC.L1-b.1.iii require regarding external connections?
flashcards.answer: Verify and control/limit connections to and use of external systems. Must identify, verify, and control both connections to external systems AND use of external systems. Includes cloud services and personal devices.

flashcards.question: What does AC.L1-b.1.iv require and who is authorized to publicly release FCI?
flashcards.answer: Controls information posted on publicly accessible systems — designated individuals, review process before posting, mechanisms to remove FCI if discovered. Only government officials can authorize public release of FCI.

flashcards.question: What is a Temporary Deficiency and how does it score?
flashcards.answer: A condition where remediation is feasible and a fix is available or in process. Must be documented in an operational plan of action with milestones showing progress. Scores as MET.

flashcards.question: What is an Enduring Exception and how does it score?
flashcards.answer: A special circumstance where full compliance is not feasible — examples include medical devices, OT, IoT, test equipment. Must be documented in the system security plan. Scores as MET.
